name: Aragorn
tagline: A Ranger without a kingdom, carrying a broken sword and a name he does not yet claim — moving through the world as if toward something just beyond the edge of his vision.

stats.note: He does not seek to charm

inPlay: Speaks quietly and directly — says what he means, wastes nothing
Always positions himself to see the door
Reads terrain and weather instinctively; often falls silent on the road
Deeply uncomfortable with cities and crowds
Wary of trust but fierce in loyalty once given
Carries a haunted quality — something unresolved behind the eyes
Skilled tracker: identifies creature, number, pace, time from prints
Gloom Stalker: invisible to darkvision; advantage on first attack in darkness
Favoured Enemy: Orcs and the servants of shadow

sections.title: About
sections.content:
He goes by Strider in the inns and wayhouses of the North. Few ask his real name. Fewer still would recognise it if they heard it. He is a Ranger — one of a dwindling brotherhood that keeps watch over lands no longer protected by any king, hunting the dark things that move at the edges of the world so that ordinary people can sleep without knowing what circles in the night beyond their fires.

He is lean and weathered, hard-used by years of outdoor living. His eyes are grey and still, the kind that have seen enough that very little surprises them anymore. He speaks little and watches much. In a crowded room he will find the seat with his back to the wall before he does anything else.

He carries a broken sword. He has never explained it.

sections.title: Appearance
sections.content:
Tall for a man, broad across the shoulders, with the kind of build that comes from hard use rather than training. His face is angular and wind-worn, dark-haired, with a short beard he keeps out of habit rather than vanity. A long scar runs along his left forearm — he does not recall every wound that left its mark, but he recalls this one.

He dresses in worn travelling clothes — dark greens and browns, a heavy cloak that has served him in all weathers. Good boots, much repaired. A longbow, a quiver, a sword that should not be as fine as it is given everything else about his appearance. He smells faintly of woodsmoke and pine.

Around his neck, on a simple chain, hangs a small token he does not speak about. He touches it sometimes without thinking, when he is tired or uncertain. Most people who notice it have the sense not to ask.

sections.title: The North
sections.content:
There are Rangers in the North. Most people have never heard of them, and those who have tend to regard them with suspicion — grim, travel-stained men and women who appear without explanation and vanish the same way, asking questions nobody wants to answer about things nobody wants to think about.

They are what remains of something older. They keep a Watch nobody appointed them to keep, over lands nobody thanks them for protecting. The Enemy has long arms and many servants, and the wilderness between the settlements of men is not as empty as it appears. Someone has to push back against what moves in the dark. The Rangers have decided, without being asked, that it will be them.

Strider is one of them. He has been one of them for as long as he can remember being anything. He does not talk about why.

sections.title: Rivendell
sections.content:
There is a place in a hidden valley that he was brought to as a child, under a name that was not his own. He was raised there for a time — educated, trained, shaped by people who understood things about him he did not yet understand about himself. He learned things there that he could not have learned anywhere else, and he left carrying a weight that the valley's peace could not diminish.

There is someone there he loves. He does not speak of her to strangers. It is not a thing he has put aside or learned to live with — it is simply something he carries, like the broken sword, without explanation.

He goes back when he can. Each time it is harder to leave.

sections.title: The Road
sections.content:
He has been walking for a long time. He knows the old roads and the ways that are no longer roads, the safe places and the places that were safe once and are no longer. He has seen things die that should not have died. He has kept things alive that the darkness would have taken. Whether the balance is even, he does not know and has learned not to calculate.

He is not unhappy. He is not happy either. He is a man with a purpose he cannot yet name, moving through the world as if toward something just beyond the edge of his vision. Something is coming. He can feel it the way he can feel weather in a change of wind.

He has been waiting a long time. He is not sure what he is waiting for. He suspects he will know it when it arrives.
